column1: What is the Artifact Kit in Cobalt Strike?
column2: A collection of stage0 payload template source code for .exe, .svc.exe, and .dll payloads that operators can modify to evade anti-virus detections

column1: What is the sole purpose of the payload artifact templates?
column2: To take Beacon shellcode and inject it into memory — they are nothing more than shellcode injectors

column1: Why are Cobalt Strike's default payload artifacts a prime target for anti-virus vendors?
column2: They are often dropped to disk and remain identical across multiple CS deployments unless specifically modified by the operator

column1: What files in the Artifact Kit src-main directory serve as the entry points for each payload type?
column2: main.c for .exe, svcmain.c for .svc.exe, and dllmain.c for .dll templates

column1: What are the bypass-*.c files in the Artifact Kit's src-common directory?
column2: Anti-sandbox technique implementations that can be selected and built into the payload template

column1: What does the Stack Spoof build option do in the Artifact Kit?
column2: Enables a stack spoofing technique to hide the fact that shellcode is executing from an unbacked (non-module-mapped) region of memory

column1: What syscall options are available when building Artifact Kit templates?
column2: none, embedded, indirect, and indirect_randomized

column1: Why should you avoid uploading custom artifacts to VirusTotal for testing?
column2: It gives the artifact away to anti-virus vendors to analyse, potentially burning the evasion technique for future use

column1: What is ThreatCheck and how is it used with artifacts?
column2: A tool that splits a binary into small chunks and scans them with Windows Defender (cloud submission disabled) to identify the smallest byte sequence detected as malicious

column1: What is Ghidra used for in the context of artifact evasion?
column2: To load the artifact, disassemble it, and locate the specific code corresponding to the malicious byte offset identified by ThreatCheck, so it can be modified in the source

column1: What is the Resource Kit in Cobalt Strike?
column2: A collection of script-based payload templates (such as PowerShell) that operators can modify — analogous to the Artifact Kit but for scripts rather than compiled executables

column1: What is AMSI?
column2: The Windows Antimalware Scan Interface — a standard that allows applications and services to submit data to the installed anti-virus product for scanning

column1: Is AMSI specific to Windows Defender?
column2: No — AMSI is vendor-agnostic, meaning any third-party anti-virus product can register itself as an AMSI provider on installation

column1: Which native Windows components are AMSI-aware?
column2: User Account Control, PowerShell, Windows Script Host (wscript/cscript), JavaScript, VBScript, and Office VBA macros

column1: What does AMSI actually do vs what the anti-virus does?
column2: AMSI is only a vehicle to submit data for scanning — it is the anti-virus product that determines whether the data is malicious, not AMSI itself

column1: What techniques are commonly used to bypass AMSI?
column2: Memory patching or hardware breakpoints to either prevent AMSI from initialising properly or to intercept scan submissions and return a fake "no threat" response

column1: Why is bypassing AMSI by patching it risky from an OPSEC perspective?
column2: Many AMSI bypasses are themselves detected, or leave indicators that a blue team can find — modifying the script to remove detectable content is a more robust approach

column1: What problem does RWX memory cause for Beacon's OPSEC?
column2: Most native applications do not use PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE memory, so its presence is a red flag that can trigger memory scanning detections

column1: What does setting stage.userwx to false in Malleable C2 cause Beacon's loader to do?
column2: Allocate memory as PAGE_READWRITE first, copy Beacon in, then loop over each DLL section and apply appropriate final permissions — RX for .text, R for .rdata, RW for .data

column1: What are Beacon's PE headers and why is their presence in memory a problem?
column2: The DOS and NT headers loaded into memory alongside Beacon make it obvious a PE is running in that memory region — they are only needed during loading, not at runtime

column1: What does setting stage.copy_pe_header to false do?
column2: Prevents the prepended loader from copying Beacon's PE headers into memory, leaving only the .text and .data sections

column1: What is module stomping (module overloading) in the context of Beacon?
column2: A technique where the loader maps a legitimate PE from disk into memory and then overwrites it with Beacon, making Beacon's memory appear to be backed by a legitimate on-disk module

column1: What requirement must be met when choosing a module for module stomping?
column2: The chosen module must be large enough to accommodate Beacon — a module of at least 512KB from System32 is recommended

column1: What are hardcoded strings in Beacon's .rdata section and why do they matter?
column2: Literal strings in the PE (such as output format strings) that can be identified by memory scanners as indicators of a Beacon in memory

column1: How can Beacon's hardcoded strings be modified for evasion?
column2: Using the strrep command in the stage.transform-x64 or stage.transform-x86 blocks of a Malleable C2 profile to replace strings — the replacement must be equal or shorter in length

column1: What is the critical constraint when replacing Beacon strings via Malleable C2?
column2: The replacement string must be shorter than or equal in length to the original, and format specifiers must remain valid (e.g. %s for strings, %d for digits)

column1: Why must strings like "HTTP/1.1 200 OK" not be replaced in Beacon's string table?
column2: Beacon uses an internal web server for workflows like powershell-import — replacing these HTTP response strings makes responses non-compliant and breaks those post-exploitation features

column1: What are the four classes of Beacon commands?
column2: Housekeeping commands, API-only commands, inline commands (BOFs), and fork & run commands

column1: What are API-only Beacon commands?
column2: Commands built directly into Beacon that rely solely on native Windows APIs — examples include file operations, token manipulation, and process management

column1: What are Beacon Object Files (BOFs) and how do they work?
column2: Compiled C programs sent to Beacon as tasking data that execute inline within the Beacon session — once finished they are cleared from memory

column1: Why are BOFs popular for extending Beacon functionality?
column2: Because they are external programs that execute inside the Beacon process, they are the most common way to add custom commands without spawning new processes

column1: What Malleable C2 block controls how BOFs and inline commands are loaded into memory?
column2: The process-inject block

column1: What is the fork & run execution pattern in Beacon?
column2: Post-exploitation capabilities implemented as DLLs are injected (appended to a loader and turned into shellcode) into a target process, with output returned to Beacon over an SMB named pipe

column1: What is the difference between Explicit and Spawn fork & run variants?
column2: Explicit injects the post-ex capability into an already-running process; Spawn starts a new sacrificial process and injects into that

column1: Why do fork & run commands carry the heaviest OPSEC burden?
column2: They must inject shellcode, start a new thread, create a named pipe, and (for Spawn) also start a new sacrificial process — all of which generate detectable events

column1: What is spawnto in Cobalt Strike?
column2: A setting that controls which process is used as the sacrificial host process when Beacon uses the Spawn fork & run variant for post-exploitation commands

column1: What is ppid spoofing in Cobalt Strike?
column2: A technique where a newly spawned process is given a spoofed parent process ID, making it appear as a child of a different process in tools that inspect process trees

column1: Why is it important to match the Beacon's host process to the C2 channel being used?
column2: To avoid suspicious process relationships — for example an HTTP/S Beacon should run inside a process that legitimately makes outbound HTTP requests, such as a browser

column1: What OPSEC problem arises when shell or run commands are run without ppid spoofing?
column2: The spawned processes appear as children of the Beacon's host process, which can be highly suspicious — for example seeing msedge.exe spawn cmd.exe

column1: What limitation exists for the spawnto directive when services create processes?
column2: The post-ex.spawnto Malleable C2 directive cannot be used for service-based payloads because environment variables like %windir% are invalid in a SYSTEM context — an explicit path must be set via the Artifact Kit's ak-settings
